title: Rally Custom Fields Architecture and Scoping
topic: Rally (CA Agile Central) custom fields architecture — TypeDefinition, AttributeDefinition, AllowedValues, RealAttributeType renderer selector, workspace vs project scoping
summary: How Rally scopes fields via TypeDefinition + AttributeDefinition + AllowedValues, and how RealAttributeType drives UI rendering — compared to Jira and Vector.
content:
Contents
  1. Overview
  2. Key Findings
  3. Detailed Analysis
  4. Comparison with Jira and Vector
  5. Implications for Vector
  6. Gaps
  7. Sources

Overview

Rally (formerly CA Agile Central, now Broadcom ValueOps Rally) manages custom fields through a three-layer API model: TypeDefinition (what artifact type the field belongs to), AttributeDefinition (the field's name, type, and constraints), and AllowedValues (the option list for constrained fields). Fields are defined at the workspace level and selectively hidden at the project level. The API prefixes all custom fields with c_ to distinguish them from system fields. The UI control rendered for a field is determined entirely by the field's RealAttributeType — a type key on the AttributeDefinition row.

Key Findings

  • Custom fields are workspace-scoped — they exist in the DB at the workspace level, not per-project
  • Visibility and required status are controlled at the workspace level; projects can only hide fields, not redefine them
  • A dropdown field must have the same option list across all projects — if different projects need different options, you must create separate fields with unique names
  • All custom fields are prefixed c_ in the WSAPI (e.g. c_SeverityLevel)
  • The RealAttributeType on AttributeDefinition is the renderer selector — it determines both storage behaviour and UI control
  • AllowedValues are workspace-scoped and must be queried with an explicit workspace parameter to avoid defaulting to the active UI workspace
  • Field type cannot be changed after creation — the type is immutable once the field exists
  • Multi-value dropdown fields store one value entry per selection (separate rows/entries, not a comma-delimited string)

Detailed Analysis

Layer 1 — TypeDefinition

Every artifact type in Rally (Defect, UserStory, Task, TestCase, PortfolioItem, etc.) is represented by a TypeDefinition object. Custom fields are attached to a TypeDefinition — they belong to an artifact type, not to a project or workspace directly. A field created for Defects is available on all Defect instances across the workspace.

GET /typedefinition?query=(Name = "Defect")
-- Returns the TypeDefinition object including its Attributes collection URL

Layer 2 — AttributeDefinition (the field definition)

A custom field is an AttributeDefinition row attached to a TypeDefinition. Three fields are required to create one:

PropertyPurpose
NameThe field identifier — prefixed c_ in API responses (e.g. c_SeverityLevel)
RealAttributeTypeThe type key — drives storage behaviour and UI control selection
TypeDefinitionThe artifact type this field belongs to (e.g. Defect)

Additional properties on AttributeDefinition:

PropertyPurpose
RequiredSet at workspace level — enforced across all open projects if true
HiddenVisibility flag — can be toggled per project to hide the field
AllowedValuesCollection URL for constrained fields (dropdown, state)
OccurrencesRead-only count of how many artifacts have a value for this field

Layer 3 — AllowedValues (options for constrained fields)

For dropdown and state fields, Rally maintains an AllowedValues collection on the AttributeDefinition. Each AllowedValue has a StringValue (the option label). The collection is workspace-scoped — different workspaces can have different option lists for the same field name, but the values must be consistent across all projects within a workspace.

GET /AttributeDefinition/<OBJECT_ID>/AllowedValues?workspace=<WORKSPACE_URL>
-- Returns:
-- { StringValue: "Submitted", _type: "AllowedAttributeValue" }
-- { StringValue: "Open",      _type: "AllowedAttributeValue" }
-- { StringValue: "Fixed",     _type: "AllowedAttributeValue" }
-- { StringValue: "Closed",    _type: "AllowedAttributeValue" }

Critical scoping note: The AllowedValues URL is not unique across workspaces. Without the explicit ?workspace= parameter, Rally defaults to the user's currently active UI workspace — which may be the wrong one. Always include the workspace parameter.

RealAttributeType — the renderer selector

The RealAttributeType value on AttributeDefinition determines both how the value is stored and which UI control is rendered. It is immutable after field creation.

RealAttributeTypeUI ControlNotes
STRINGSingle-line text inputMax 256 chars. Accepts alphanumeric + special chars.
TEXTRich text / multi-lineUp to 32 KB. Supports limited HTML tags.
INTEGERNumber inputMax 9 digits.
DECIMALDecimal number inputNumeric with decimal precision.
BOOLEANCheckboxChecked = true, unchecked = false.
DATEDate pickerCalendar icon in editors. Cannot display on cards.
DROPDOWNSingle-select dropdownOptions from AllowedValues. Same list across all projects in workspace.
MULTI_VALUE_DROPDOWNMulti-select dropdownMultiple values stored as separate entries.
WEB_LINKURL inputEmbeds a reference to an external system object.
STATEState selectorLike DROPDOWN but drives workflow state transitions.
OBJECTObject reference pickerReference to another Rally artifact (lookup field).

The UI never decides what control to render — it reads RealAttributeType from the AttributeDefinition and picks the matching component from a registry. This is the direct equivalent of Jira's CUSTOMFIELDTYPEKEY plugin lookup and Vector's type column on the schema row.

Workspace vs Project scoping

Rally's scoping model is simpler than Jira's context system:

ConcernWhere controlledNotes
Field existenceWorkspace levelAll custom fields are created at workspace scope
Field requiredWorkspace level onlyCannot make a field required in only one project
Field visibilityWorkspace default; project can hideProjects can hide workspace fields but cannot add new ones independently
Allowed valuesWorkspace levelSame option list for all projects — no per-project customisation
Standard fieldsWorkspace level onlySystem fields can only be shown/hidden at workspace level, not project level

Key constraint: If two projects need different dropdown options for "Severity", they cannot share a field — they need separate fields with different names. This is a significant limitation compared to Jira's context model (which allows per-project option lists on the same field) and compared to Vector's UNIQUE(subscription_id, field_name) model (which allows per-workspace option lists).

c_ prefix convention

All custom fields appear in WSAPI responses with a c_ prefix on their element name. A field created as SeverityLevel appears in API responses as c_SeverityLevel. This is a stable convention across all Rally WSAPI versions — custom app authors use it to distinguish custom fields from system fields in API responses without needing to query the AttributeDefinition catalogue first.

Field creation via WSAPI

POST /AttributeDefinition
{
  "AttributeDefinition": {
    "Name": "c_SeverityLevel",
    "RealAttributeType": "DROPDOWN",
    "TypeDefinition": { "_ref": "/typedefinition/12345" }
  }
}

After creation, AllowedValues are added separately via POST to the AllowedValues collection on the new AttributeDefinition.

Comparison with Jira and Vector

ConcernRallyJiraVector (proposed)
Field definition scopeWorkspace — one definition per field per workspaceGlobal — one definition per Jira instanceWorkspace — one row per field per subscription_id
Per-project optionsNo — same options across all projectsYes — per-context option listsYes — options_json is per subscription_id row
Renderer selectorRealAttributeType string keyCUSTOMFIELDTYPEKEY plugin IDtype TEXT column on schema row
Options storageSeparate AllowedValues collectioncustomfieldoption table per contextoptions_json JSONB on schema row
Value storagePer-artifact, one row per valuecustomfieldvalue EAV (typed columns)_field_values EAV (typed columns)
Custom field prefixc_ in APIcustomfield_ in APIfield_name (no prefix, subscription-scoped)
Type mutabilityImmutable after creationImmutable after creationImmutable after first value written (proposed)
Multi-value fieldsMULTI_VALUE_DROPDOWN — multiple entriesMultiple customfieldvalue rowsMultiple _field_values rows, same field_name

Implications for Vector

  • Vector's per-workspace options are better than Rally's — Rally forces the same option list across all projects in a workspace; Vector's options_json per subscription_id row allows Workspace A and B to have different severity options independently. This is a genuine UX advantage.
  • The c_ prefix pattern is worth considering — Rally's c_ prefix lets API consumers distinguish custom fields from system fields instantly. Vector could adopt a similar convention in Samantha API responses: system fields (title, owner_id, etc.) have no prefix; custom schema fields appear as f_severity or similar.
  • Type immutability should be enforced — both Rally and Jira make field type immutable after creation. Vector should enforce this once a field has value rows — changing type would invalidate stored values. Add a service-layer guard: reject type updates if any field_values rows exist for that field_name + subscription_id.
  • Workspace parameter pattern — Rally's AllowedValues scoping bug (defaults to active UI workspace without explicit parameter) is a cautionary tale. Vector's Samantha SDK must always resolve subscription_id from the authenticated session — never allow callers to omit it or default it.

Gaps

  • Rally's internal DB schema for storing field values is not publicly documented — the above is inferred from WSAPI behaviour
  • The complete enumeration of RealAttributeType values is not in public docs — STRING, TEXT, INTEGER, DECIMAL, BOOLEAN, DATE, DROPDOWN, MULTI_VALUE_DROPDOWN, WEB_LINK, STATE, OBJECT are confirmed from community sources
  • How Rally handles field schema migrations (renaming, archiving) is not covered in available docs
  • Performance characteristics at large scale (millions of AllowedValue rows) not documented

Sources

  1. Broadcom TechDocs — Rally: Manage Custom Fields
  2. Broadcom Knowledge — How to create a custom field using WSAPI
  3. Broadcom Knowledge — Get AllowedValues for Rally dropdown fields
  4. Broadcom TechDocs — Rally: View Fields
  5. Broadcom TechDocs — Rally Web Services API reference
